methodology_reference: Adapted from Semigran et al. (BMJ, 2015; doi:10.1136/bmj.h3480) and the Veterinary Triage List (Ruys et al., 2012) / VetTriS (Groesser et al., 2025; doi:10.1111/vec.70068).

triage_categories.EMERGENCY: Requires immediate veterinary attention (minutes). Life-threatening if untreated. Equivalent to VTL Red/Orange (0-15 min target wait).
triage_categories.URGENT: Should see a vet same day or within 24h. Not immediately life-threatening but risk of deterioration. Equivalent to VTL Yellow (30-60 min).
triage_categories.SELF_CARE: Monitor at home, routine vet appointment if persists >48-72h. Equivalent to VTL Green (120+ min / elective).

scoring.exact_match: 1 point if app triage exactly matches ground_truth_category
scoring.safe_overtriage: 0 points but NOT penalised (SELF_CARE→URGENT or URGENT→EMERGENCY)
scoring.unsafe_undertriage: -1 point AND flagged as 'unsafe' (EMERGENCY→URGENT/SELF_CARE or URGENT→SELF_CARE when presenting with red-flag symptom)

safety_metric: Unsafe undertriage rate (% of vignettes where system fails to escalate appropriately)

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Gastric Dilatation and Volvulus in Small Animals
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog is retching and can't vomit anything, his belly looks huge and tight, he can't get comfortable.
red_flags: distended abdomen + unproductive retching in large breed = GDV until proven otherwise
vignettes.vet_rationale: GDV is rapidly fatal without emergency surgery (gastric decompression ± gastropexy). Mortality rises steeply with delay.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Urethral Obstruction in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My male cat keeps going to the litter box but nothing comes out, he's crying and now he's vomited twice.
red_flags: male cat + straining + no urine output = obstructed until proven otherwise
vignettes.vet_rationale: Urethral obstruction causes rapid azotemia, hyperkalaemia, and cardiac arrhythmia. Fatal within 24-48h if untreated.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Xylitol Toxicosis in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog ate a pack of sugar-free gum about half an hour ago. She's vomited once and seems wobbly.
red_flags: xylitol = hypoglycaemia + hepatic necrosis in dogs
vignettes.vet_rationale: Xylitol causes acute hypoglycaemia within 30-60 min and potentially fatal hepatic necrosis at higher doses. Decontamination window is narrow.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Acute haemoabdomen – suspected splenic mass rupture
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Haemoabdomen; WSAVA Emergency Guidelines
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog suddenly collapsed in the garden, his gums are white, his belly looks swollen. He got up briefly but collapsed again.
red_flags: pale gums + collapse + abdominal distension = haemoabdomen/shock
vignettes.vet_rationale: Haemoabdomen (often ruptured splenic haemangiosarcoma) causes hypovolaemic shock. Without emergency surgery survival is minutes to hours.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Acute respiratory distress – congestive heart failure / pleural effusion
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Pleural Effusion in Cats; Feline Cardiomyopathy
condensed_findings: sitting upright refusing to lie down
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat is breathing with her mouth open, her neck is stretched out and she won't lie down. Her gums look a little blue.
red_flags: open-mouth breathing in a cat = emergency; blue/cyanotic gums = critical hypoxia
vignettes.vet_rationale: Cats almost never breathe through their mouths unless critically dyspnoeic. Pleural effusion or pulmonary oedema requires immediate oxygen, diuresis, or drainage.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Seizure Disorders in Dogs; Ilie & Thomovsky 2024
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog is having a seizure right now, it started about 8 minutes ago and he hasn't stopped. He peed himself.
red_flags: seizure >5 minutes = status epilepticus; risk of hyperthermia, brain damage, respiratory compromise
vignettes.vet_rationale: Status epilepticus (seizure >5 min) requires immediate IV benzodiazepines. Mortality rises sharply with delay.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Aortic Thromboembolism in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat suddenly screamed and now he can't use his back legs at all. They're cold and his paw pads look blue.
red_flags: acute paralysis + cold + cyanotic limbs = ATE; extreme pain
vignettes.vet_rationale: Feline ATE causes acute ischaemic neuromyopathy. Pain management and cardiology assessment are emergency priorities; prognosis varies.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Abdominal Trauma; WSAVA Emergency
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog ran into a broken fence post. There's a wound in her belly and I can see something coming out of it. She's very pale.
red_flags: evisceration + shock = surgical emergency
vignettes.vet_rationale: Penetrating abdominal trauma with evisceration requires immediate surgery. Risk of peritonitis, sepsis, and haemorrhage.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Acute toxicity – permethrin (spot-on for dogs applied to cat)
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Permethrin Toxicosis in Cats
condensed_findings: owner applied dog flea treatment to cat
vignettes.presenting_complaint: I accidentally put my dog's flea drops on my cat two hours ago. She's now shaking and drooling a lot.
red_flags: permethrin = severe neurotoxicity in cats; rapidly progressive
vignettes.vet_rationale: Permethrin causes neurological signs (tremors, seizures) rapidly. Requires urgent decontamination and anticonvulsant therapy. Fatality without treatment.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Acute vestibular syndrome with vomiting and head tilt
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Vestibular Syndrome; Ilie & Thomovsky 2024 (Part II)
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog suddenly has his head tilted way to one side, he keeps rolling and can't stand up, and he's been sick several times.
red_flags: acute vestibular signs can mimic stroke; inability to stand + nystagmus requires same-day assessment to rule out central (CNS) cause
vignettes.vet_rationale: Acute vestibular syndrome (peripheral or central). Central causes (brain tumour, inflammatory) require urgent imaging. Even peripheral requires supportive care for vomiting/aspiration risk.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Urolithiasis in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My Dalmatian keeps squatting to wee but only a few drops come out, he's whimpering, and now he's vomited.
red_flags: near-complete obstruction in male dog = emergency; Dalmatians predisposed to urate uroliths
vignettes.vet_rationale: Urethral obstruction in dogs causes azotaemia, electrolyte imbalance, bladder rupture risk. Requires catheterisation or surgery urgently.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Diabetic Ketoacidosis in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My diabetic cat hasn't eaten in two days and is vomiting. She's barely moving and her breath smells sweet/fruity.
red_flags: known diabetic + vomiting + sweet breath = DKA until proven otherwise
vignettes.vet_rationale: DKA is a metabolic emergency requiring IV fluids, electrolyte correction, and insulin management. Fatal if untreated.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Haemorrhagic Gastroenteritis in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog has had bloody diarrhoea about six times in the last two hours and vomited blood. She looks weak and pale.
red_flags: haematochezia + haematemesis + weakness + pallor = AHDS with haemoconcentration, potential shock
vignettes.vet_rationale: AHDS can cause PCV >60% and hypovolaemic shock rapidly. IV fluid resuscitation is usually life-saving if started early.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Urethral obstruction in female cat (rare but possible)
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My female cat has been in the litter box all morning straining but I see no urine, just a tiny bit of blood. She's vomited once.
red_flags: obstruction rarer in females but possible; vomiting + no urine = obstruction suspected
vignettes.vet_rationale: Urethral obstruction in female cats is uncommon but occurs. Requires same-day evaluation given azotaemia risk.

vignettes.breed_hint: Staffordshire Bull Terrier, 4 years, male neutered
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Anticoagulant Rodenticide Poisoning
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog ate rat poison two days ago. Now he's coughing up blood, his gums are white, and I can see bruises under his skin.
red_flags: anticoagulant poisoning causes delayed coagulopathy 2-5 days post-ingestion; active haemorrhage = emergency
vignettes.vet_rationale: Anticoagulant rodenticides deplete Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Active haemorrhage requires plasma transfusion, Vitamin K1, and oxygen support.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Cranial Cruciate Ligament Rupture in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog was playing and suddenly yelped and won't put her back leg down. There's no visible wound but she won't bear weight.
red_flags: non-weight-bearing = same day evaluation; neurological exam needed
vignettes.vet_rationale: CCL rupture is common in large breeds. Not immediately life-threatening but requires same-day evaluation to confirm diagnosis and plan management.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Urinary tract infection / feline idiopathic cystitis (FIC)
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Feline Idiopathic Cystitis
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My male cat has been going to the litter box frequently, produces a small amount of blood-tinged urine each time. He's eating and seems OK otherwise.
red_flags: male cat: must confirm producing some urine (partial obstruction vs FIC)
vignettes.vet_rationale: FIC in male cats must be differentiated from early/partial obstruction. Same-day evaluation needed to exclude urethral obstruction; urinalysis required.

vignettes.breed_hint: Cavalier King Charles Spaniel, 8 years, male neutered
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Myxomatous Mitral Valve Disease in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My Cavalier has always had a heart murmur but his cough is suddenly much worse, especially at night, and he can't walk as far as he used to.
red_flags: known cardiac disease + worsening cough = possible early pulmonary oedema; same-day needed
vignettes.vet_rationale: MMVD can decompensate. Pulmonary oedema requires prompt diuretic adjustment. Gums remain pink currently but could deteriorate.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Hypoglycaemia in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My diabetic cat had her insulin this morning but ate less than usual. Now she's walking strangely and seems confused but is still awake.
red_flags: mild hypoglycaemia: if owner can give oral glucose (Karo syrup on gums) and cat responds, still needs same-day vet for glucose check
vignettes.vet_rationale: Mild hypoglycaemia can be temporarily reversed at home but requires same-day blood glucose check and insulin dose adjustment.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Acute onset hindlimb weakness / degenerative myelopathy or disc disease
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Intervertebral Disc Disease; Degenerative Myelopathy
condensed_findings: no pain response on hind paws
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog's back legs have gotten weaker over a few hours. She's dragging her back feet and I don't think she can feel me pinching her paws.
red_flags: reduced/absent pain sensation = risk of permanent paralysis; same-day imaging needed
vignettes.vet_rationale: Spinal cord compromise needs same-day MRI/CT to determine if surgical decompression is indicated. Prognosis depends on speed of intervention.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Suspected bite wound abscess – systemically unwell
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Bite Wound Management
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat has a swollen lump on her neck that feels like it's filled with fluid. She has a temperature, won't eat and seems unwell.
red_flags: fever + systemic signs + bite wound = same-day evaluation; risk of septicaemia if abscess not drained
vignettes.vet_rationale: Cat bite abscesses with systemic signs require drainage and antibiotics. FIV/FeLV transmission from bite wounds also warrants testing.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Proptosis (eye popped out of socket) – recent
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Proptosis in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My Pug had a fight with another dog and his eye has come out of the socket. It happened about 30 minutes ago and the eye looks moist.
red_flags: proptosis in brachycephalic dog is time-sensitive; preservation of vision depends on rapid replacement
vignettes.vet_rationale: Proptosis requires emergency treatment to preserve the eye. Brachycephalic breeds are highly susceptible due to shallow orbits.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Suspected hepatic lipidosis – not eating 3 days
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Hepatic Lipidosis in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My overweight cat stopped eating completely 3 days ago. He looks slightly yellow around the eyes and is very lethargic.
red_flags: anorexia >48-72h in obese cat = hepatic lipidosis developing; same-day evaluation
vignettes.vet_rationale: Feline hepatic lipidosis is preventable if nutrition support starts early; fatal if untreated. Jaundice confirms hepatocellular involvement.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Pancreatitis in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog ate a lot of fatty sausages yesterday. Since last night she's been vomiting, hunching over and won't move much. She seems uncomfortable.
red_flags: acute pancreatitis with pain: same-day evaluation for IV fluids and analgesia; risk of necrotising pancreatitis
vignettes.vet_rationale: Pancreatitis ranges from mild to severe/necrotising. Persistent vomiting + pain warrants same-day hospitalisation for fluids and pain control.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Traumatic injury after fall – suspected internal trauma
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Trauma Assessment in Cats; High-Rise Syndrome
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat fell from our 4th-floor balcony. She's up and walking but limping and her belly seems sore. She's breathing a bit fast.
red_flags: high-rise syndrome: occult thoracic injury, pneumothorax, organ rupture risk even if ambulatory
vignettes.vet_rationale: Cats after high-rise falls need same-day evaluation for pneumothorax, diaphragmatic hernia, bladder rupture, spinal injury. Shock can develop with delay.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Intervertebral Disc Disease in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My Dachshund won't jump onto the sofa anymore and walked stiffly this morning. He seems to be in back pain but can still walk.
red_flags: ambulatory IVDD: urgent but not emergency if still walking; can progress to paralysis without treatment
vignettes.vet_rationale: Ambulatory IVDD in Dachshunds requires same-day evaluation and strict rest. Risk of rapid progression to non-ambulatory status.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Eye injury – corneal ulceration with discharge
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Corneal Ulceration in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat is squinting his right eye, there's yellowish discharge and the eye looks cloudy. He keeps pawing at it.
red_flags: corneal ulcer can perforate within 24-48h if untreated, especially with discharge
vignettes.vet_rationale: Corneal ulceration requires same-day slit-lamp examination. Fluorescein staining needed. Risk of perforation and endophthalmitis.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Ingestion of foreign body – suspected obstruction
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Gastric and Intestinal Foreign Bodies in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My Labrador ate a corn cob yesterday. She's been vomiting since then, hasn't passed any stool and won't eat.
red_flags: corn cob = high obstruction risk; persistent vomiting 24h post-ingestion = likely obstruction forming
vignettes.vet_rationale: GI foreign body obstruction requires same-day X-ray. Surgical removal is usually required; delay risks intestinal necrosis or perforation.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Hyperthyroidism with acute signs – hypertensive crisis
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Hyperthyroidism in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My hyperthyroid cat is suddenly very restless, yowling and seems to not be able to see properly. She's on her medication.
red_flags: vision loss in hypertensive cat = hypertensive retinopathy/retinal detachment; same-day urgent
vignettes.vet_rationale: Hypertensive crisis in cats (often secondary to hyperthyroidism or CKD) can cause acute retinal detachment and permanent blindness. Urgent BP measurement and medication needed.

vignettes.breed_hint: Jack Russell Terrier, 5 years, male neutered
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Canine Parvovirus
condensed_findings: contact with unvaccinated puppy 5 days ago
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My unvaccinated dog has had bloody diarrhoea and vomiting since yesterday. He played with an unvaccinated puppy about 5 days ago.
red_flags: unvaccinated + bloody diarrhoea + contact history = parvovirus suspected; needs isolation + urgent supportive care
vignettes.vet_rationale: Parvovirus causes severe haemorrhagic gastroenteritis; fatal without IV fluids and supportive care. Isolation protocols essential.

vignettes.breed_hint: Cavalier King Charles Spaniel, 6 years, female spayed
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Gastroenteritis in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog ate a lot of grass this morning and vomited once. She had one soft stool but is playing normally, drinking and has pink gums.
vignettes.vet_rationale: Single episode of vomiting + soft stool after grass eating with normal mentation, drinking, and pink gums = dietary indiscretion. Monitor 24-48h; withhold food 4-6h then bland diet.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Hairballs in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat vomited up a hairball this morning. She ate her breakfast straight after and is acting completely normally.
vignettes.vet_rationale: Occasional hairball vomiting is normal in cats. Normal behaviour, appetite, and hydration afterwards: no veterinary visit needed. Recommend hairball prevention (regular grooming, hairball paste/diet).

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Mild allergic skin reaction – suspected insect bite
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Hypersensitivity Disorders in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog got a small hive on his face after being outside. His muzzle is slightly puffy but it's not spreading. He's playing normally and breathing fine.
red_flags: monitor for progression to anaphylaxis: difficulty breathing, collapse, vomiting, rapid spread = upgrade to EMERGENCY
vignettes.vet_rationale: Mild, non-progressive urticaria/angioedema limited to face: monitor closely at home. If spreads, breathing changes, or vomiting starts → emergency.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Sneezing – mild upper respiratory tract infection
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Feline Upper Respiratory Tract Infections
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat has been sneezing for two days with a little clear nasal discharge. He's eating and drinking well and seems perfectly bright.
red_flags: monitor for: not eating, thick/yellow discharge, eye changes, breathing difficulty = upgrade to URGENT
vignettes.vet_rationale: Mild URTI with clear discharge, normal appetite/hydration: supportive care at home (humid environment, steam). Vet visit if not improved in 5-7 days or signs worsen.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Wound Management
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog cut her paw on something in the garden. The bleeding stopped after I applied pressure. It's a small clean cut, she's walking normally.
red_flags: if wound >2cm, deep, gaping, or won't stop bleeding = URGENT
vignettes.vet_rationale: Small, clean, superficial wounds with bleeding controlled: clean gently with saline, apply non-adherent dressing. Monitor for infection. Routine vet check if not healed in 5-7 days.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Constipation in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My young cat hasn't passed a stool since yesterday. She's straining a little in the litter box but is eating normally and seems fine otherwise.
red_flags: monitor for: producing nothing at all, multiple episodes, blood, pain, vomiting = upgrade; and verify is stool not urine straining
vignettes.vet_rationale: Mild single-episode constipation in young otherwise healthy cat: ensure hydration, add pumpkin/fibre. If no stool in 48h or signs worsen, seek vet advice.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Musculoskeletal Disorders in Dogs
condensed_findings: mild limp on front right leg
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog is limping slightly on his front right leg after a long game of fetch. He's still putting weight on it and there's no swelling.
red_flags: non-weight-bearing or swelling/deformity = upgrade to URGENT
vignettes.vet_rationale: Weight-bearing soft-tissue strain after exercise: rest 24-48h. If not improved or worsens, routine vet appointment. No emergency required.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Feline Dermatology
condensed_findings: no recent changes except new washing powder
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat has been over-grooming her belly and there's a thin patch of fur. No sores or redness, just excessive licking. She's otherwise completely normal.
vignettes.vet_rationale: Psychogenic alopecia or contact irritation (new cleaning product): remove potential irritant. Routine vet visit if persists >2 weeks or skin becomes inflamed.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Diarrhoea in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog has had soft stools today. We changed his food to a new brand two days ago. He's eating, drinking and playing normally.
vignettes.vet_rationale: Soft stool after food change: transition gradually over 7 days. Withhold new food, return to old. Probiotics helpful. Vet only if persists >3-4 days or blood appears.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Vaccination Guidelines; WSAVA Vaccination Guidelines
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat had his booster vaccine this morning and seems a bit quiet and less interested in food this afternoon. He's eaten a little.
red_flags: watch for: facial swelling, vomiting within 1h, collapse, difficulty breathing = anaphylaxis = EMERGENCY
vignettes.vet_rationale: Mild lethargy and reduced appetite 24h post-vaccination is a normal, expected reaction. No action needed unless signs of anaphylaxis develop.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Anal Sac Disease in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog has been scooting his bottom along the carpet and licking his rear. He's otherwise completely normal.
red_flags: if swelling, abscess, or blood visible = URGENT
vignettes.vet_rationale: Anal gland impaction: routine expression by vet or groomer. Not an emergency. Routine appointment within a few days.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Mild conjunctivitis – one eye, clear discharge
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Conjunctivitis in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat has a watery, slightly red eye. Just one eye, no cloudiness. She's eating and acting normally.
red_flags: if cloudy cornea, squinting severely, or yellow discharge = URGENT
vignettes.vet_rationale: Mild unilateral serous conjunctivitis: routine vet appointment within 3-5 days. Clean gently with saline. If cornea involved or signs worsen: upgrade.

vignettes.ground_truth_condition: Ear scratching – mild otitis externa without systemic signs
vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Otitis Externa in Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My dog has been scratching at his ear and I can smell something. He's not in obvious distress and is otherwise fine.
red_flags: if severe pain, head tilt, or pus = URGENT
vignettes.vet_rationale: Mild otitis externa: routine vet appointment within a week for ear examination and cleaning. Not an emergency.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Nail Disorders in Cats
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My cat broke a nail. There's no bleeding, he's walking normally and just occasionally licks the paw.
red_flags: if nail is hanging/attached and bleeding = URGENT for removal
vignettes.vet_rationale: Broken nail without bleeding or exposed quick: clean gently. Routine vet visit if concerned about infection. Not an emergency.

vignettes.source_reference: MSD Veterinary Manual – Respiratory Disorders of Brachycephalic Dogs
vignettes.presenting_complaint: My French Bulldog made a weird snorting/honking sound for about 30 seconds, like he was trying to breathe in. Then it just stopped and he's fine now.
red_flags: frequent episodes or cyanosis = URGENT; single brief episode = normal variant in brachycephalic breeds
vignettes.vet_rationale: Reverse sneezing is common in brachycephalic breeds and usually benign. Single brief episode with spontaneous resolution and normal behaviour: no action needed. Discuss with vet at next routine visit.
